data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: FCC Router Ban Plugs Into Gains
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

U.S. bans new foreign-made routers over security risks. (00:14) SK hynix (HXSCL) to buy $7.9B equipment from ASML. (01:20) Puig (PUIGF) stock jumps after Estée Lauder (EL) confirms merger talks. (01:57)

This is an abridged transcript.

Netgear (NTGR) is on our biggest movers list. NTGR is up 14%.

Shares surged after the Federal Communications Commission added foreign-made Wi-Fi routers to its “Covered List,” effectively banning the import of new models on national security grounds.

The FCC said in a statement that the foreign-made routers introduce “a supply chain vulnerability that could disrupt the U.S. economy, critical infrastructure, and national defense” and pose “a severe cybersecurity risk that could be leveraged to immediately and severely disrupt US critical infrastructure.”

The move targets future devices while allowing existing inventory and installed routers to remain in use.

The impact will extend beyond Chinese players such as TP-Link, affecting U.S.-headquartered firms as well.

Companies including Netgear (NTGR), Eero (AMZN), and Google (GOOG) Nest design their products domestically but depend heavily on manufacturing bases in Asia.

SK hynix (HXSCL) is acquiring advanced production equipment valued at 12 trillion won ($7.9 billion) from Dutch company ASML (ASML), aiming to meet rising demand for memory chips.

The South Korean chipmaker announced the decision through a regulatory filing. Reports say the extreme ultraviolet scanners will be delivered by December 2027 for its next-generation production processes.

The move comes amid SK Hynix's push to expand the production of memory chips, including high bandwidth memory, on the back of soaring demand from the artificial intelligence industry.

According to a separate report, SK Hynix (HXSCL) is seeking to raise 10 trillion won to 15 trillion won ($10 billion) from a potential listing in the US, the Korea Economic Daily reported.

The company is currently the main HBM supplier to Nvidia (NVDA), although Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) and Micron (MU) supply it with smaller volumes.

Shares of Spanish beauty group Puig (PUIGF) jumped on Tuesday after Estée Lauder (EL) confirmed it is in talks about merging the two companies.

Puig (PUIGF), which is behind brands such as Rabanne and Charlotte Tilbury, has a market cap of EU8.8 billion ($10.3B). Estee Lauder has a market value of $31B.

Puig’s Spain-listed shares jumped as much as 15% on Tuesday, while Estée Lauder (EL) was up 0.6% in U.S. premarket trading.

This is the top story in the Wall Street Breakfast newsletter. Here’s a link to sign up.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

OpenAI flags Microsoft dependence as key business risk ahead of expected IPO - report

Broadcom exec says Taiwan Semiconductor is reaching capacity limits - report

Crude climbs on supply worries after Iran rejects U.S. negotiation claims

Catalyst watch:

  • Shareholders with Two Harbors Investment (TWO) will vote on the deal to be acquired by mortgage wholesaler UWM Holdings (UWMC).

  • JFB Construction (JFB) will begin trading on a split-adjusted basis following the two-for-one stock split.

  • The ShopTalk conference in Las Vegas will include a keynote from Reddit (RDDT) CEO Steve Huffman. Top execs from Wayfair (W), Stitch Fix (SFIX), Pinterest (PINS), Gap (GAP), Dutch Bros (BROS), and SharkNinja (SN) will also appear.

  • MercadoLibre (MELI) will appear at a Morgan Stanley conference.

  • Zillow Group (ZG) will host an investor event focused on the company's AI innovation in real estate.

  • CrowdStrike (CRWD) CEO George Kurtz will give a keynote address at the RSA security conference.

  • Asure Software's (ASUR) CEO will participate in a panel discussion on AI and Implications for Business Services and Software at the Roth Conference.

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is up 2.5% at $90. Bitcoin is up 0.5% at $71,000. Gold is up 0.4% at $4,425.

The FTSE 100 is little changed and the DAX is down 0.2%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Outlook Therapeutics (OTLK) -25% - Shares plunged after the company announced a best-efforts public offering of common stock and accompanying warrants, with potential inclusion of pre-funded warrants.

Economic calendar:

  • 9:45 am PMI Composite Flash

  • 10:00 am New Home Sales

data.title: ClearBridge Dividend Strategy Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: 2025 marked the third consecutive year of this AI-driven cycle. The market cap-weighted S&P 500 Index rose 17.9% in the year, while the equal-weighted S&P 500 Index gained just 11.4%. The Magnificent Seven, this market's nucleus, rose 24.9% in 2025. While performance comparisons will vary based on the time period, cap-weighted and equal-weighted indexes have produced similar returns over long periods of time.
  • Contributors: Stock selection in consumer staples, a health care overweight and IT underweight proved beneficial.
  • Detractors: Quarterly underperformance was driven primarily by stock selection in the communication services, IT, materials and real estate sectors.
  • Outlook: We anticipate the AI debate will carry over in 2026, and we will continue to participate in AI in a measured and disciplined way. Yet, as other investors continue to myopically focus on AI, we expect to find additional idiosyncratic opportunities in overlooked corners of the market. We believe these seeds will bear fruit in the years to come.

Performance Review

  • The S&P 500 managed a 2.7% gain in the fourth quarter, while the Strategy captured roughly half of that.
  • On the plus side, consumer staples holdings such as Nestle (NSRGF), Coca-Cola (KO) and Unilever (UL) and a health care overweight shined as concerns of a tech bubble grew.
  • On the downside, we are underweight Alphabet (GOOGL) and Apple (AAPL), two Magnificent Seven stocks that performed well, and overweight Oracle (ORCL), which gave back some of its large gains from earlier in 2025. Increasing competitive dynamics in the wireless industry also weighed on T-Mobile (TMUS).
  • 2025 marked the third consecutive year of this AI-driven cycle. The market cap-weighted S&P 500 Index rose 17.9% in the year, while the equal-weighted S&P 500 Index gained just 11.4%. The Magnificent Seven, this market's nucleus, rose 24.9% in 2025. Since ChatGPT launched three years ago, the cap-weighted S&P 500 has delivered nearly twice the gains of its equal-weighted peer. The Magnificent Seven, meanwhile, has surged a staggering 332%.
  • While performance comparisons will vary based on the time period, cap-weighted and equal-weighted indexes have produced similar returns over long periods of time.
  • In 2025, and throughout the last three years, the ClearBridge Dividend Strategy has enjoyed healthy gains, in part due to its investments in AI participants like Alphabet, Broadcom, Meta Platforms, Microsoft and Oracle.
  • We have not gained as much as the cap-weighted S&P 500, our benchmark, however, as we maintain diversification to prevent the portfolio from becoming overly concentrated.
  • Market conditions in 2025 provided opportunities in companies we have long followed and admired such as: Automatic Data Processing (ADP)(a fourth-quarter addition), Inditex, L3Harris (LHX), Marsh & McLennan (MRSH), Old Dominion Freight Lines (ODFL) and TE Connectivity (TEL). We also took advantage of situations in select existing holdings to significantly increase our positions in Air Products (APD), Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Union Pacific (UNP). All represent high-quality franchises in attractive industries underwritten based on reasonable cash returns. It is an eclectic bunch, sourced from bottom-up analysis, not a trend-following, top-down approach.
  • Given our approach to investing — conservative, diversified and risk-averse — AI is not central to our portfolio. Indeed, no one sector or trend will ever be central to the ClearBridge Dividend Strategy.
  • In momentum-driven, concentrated markets, diversification inherently impacts relative performance comparisons. Of course, it also limits losses when that trade, inevitably, unwinds.

Outlook

  • We anticipate the AI debate will carry over in 2026, and we will continue to participate in AI in a measured and disciplined way. Yet, as other investors continue to myopically focus on AI, we expect to find additional idiosyncratic opportunities in overlooked corners of the market. We believe these seeds will bear fruit in the years to come. The Strategy, meanwhile, continues to trade at a significant discount to the broader market while exhibiting robust dividend growth on average. With market concentration and valuations at or near all-time highs, we remain committed to diversification and valuation discipline. These principles have served investors well in the past; we believe they will continue to serve us well in the years ahead.

data.title: You're Up 150% On Planet Labs, What To Do Now?
data.description:
Thesis

Planet Labs PBC (PL) delivered a particularly strong 4Q26 beat, and the stock saw significant upside of about 25%. Revenue came in at $86.8 million, a figure that exceeded expectations by about $8.7 million, while the non-GAAP EPS was $0.00, again beating the consensus by $0.05. So there is clearly accelerating demand momentum here. You can also see that in the guidance, 1Q27, the company expects $87 million to $91 million in revenue with an adjusted EBITDA loss of just $3 million to $6 million. The FY27 guidance is calling for $415 million to $440 million in revenue but only $0 to $10 million in EBITDA.

Now, since first initiating my coverage on Planet, shares are up about 162%. So a lot of investors are thinking about whether they should take profits. This is especially true when you consider just how aggressively this stock is now valued. Therefore, going forward, the question to answer is, can Planet justify a valuation that already prices in major success? Personally, I've been quite bullish on the whole satellite surveillance industry, favoring companies that first have the satellite coverage, but specifically those that are innovative in the space. Now, that innovative side could come from improving techniques like revisit rates and resolution or from AI integration. And in terms of building a diversified satellite portfolio, in which different clients can choose satellite coverage based on their needs, Planet is certainly leading the race. So in the longer term, I definitely see more upside to the stock and the industry as a whole. However, in the short term, investors should be aware of just how big the new expectations are on Planet and that we could see more short-term volatility.

FY26 overview

It’s safe to say that we saw some pretty strong top-line expansion from Planet. We also saw a meaningful shift toward operational maturity. Revenue growth was quite robust, up about 26% year-over-year to $307.7 million, with an especially strong figure for 4Q26 at $86.8 million. I simply see this as evidence of accelerating demand for their satellite data and analytics services. Now, what's even more telling is the surge in contracted future business. Their remaining performance obligations more than doubled to a hefty $852 million, and the backlog surpassed $900 million. That backlog figure is up about 79% since last year. So clearly, there is some high revenue visibility and sustained demand. I particularly saw this coming from government and defence customers such as Sweden, NATO, and U.S. defence agencies. If you think about it, the fact that 98% of annual contract value is recurring just goes to show how durable Planet’s business model is here. I’ve always viewed it more as a subscription data provider than a traditional aerospace company.

In terms of profitability level, the picture is a bit more nuanced but still very much improving under the surface. That being said, on the surface, we can see a pretty large GAAP net loss of $246.9 million for the year, but we have to keep in mind that this was heavily distorted by non-cash charges. Specifically, there was about $161.4 million tied to the fair value revaluation of warrant liabilities due to stock price movements. So if we strip these out, Planet would then achieve a key milestone: positive adjusted EBITDA of about $15.5 million for FY26. This is actually one of the main reasons I would remain buy-rated on Planet in the long term, in that profitability is getting closer. We can also compare this adjusted EBITDA to a loss in the prior year, along with $52.9 million in free cash flow and $134 million in operating cash flow. So what simply tells me is that the core business is now cash-generative.

As for margins, these are still somewhat pressured, and if you look across the surveillance industry of those using subscription services, margins have traditionally been an issue. Planet’s gross margin is slightly declining at 56% vs. 57% last year, which would indicate rising costs. There was an increase in operating expenses of $83 million QoQ. If you understand the nature of the business, I think this is inevitable due to just how costly satellite deployment is. That being said, the company still ended with a significantly strengthened balance sheet. They now hold about $640 million in cash and short-term investments, a figure that is up 188% year over year, and this should give them ample flexibility to invest. I see the main investment areas in CY26 being AI and next-generation satellite systems. I’ve covered their innovative side in my previous coverage.

So, for specific growth vectors here, it's now clear Planet is doubling down in two major areas: sovereign/defence satellite services and AI-driven analytics. We saw the expansion of large, multi-year government contracts and partnerships, including that notable agreement with Sweden and ongoing work with NATO and U.S. agencies. So they want to go after higher-value, long-duration deals. But at the same time, management is also setting up a nice position in AI as the next growth engine. The goal here is to extract more value from its already pretty vast Earth observation dataset. In terms of guidance for FY27, it is pretty ambitious, with revenue expected to fall between $415 million and $440 million, so again, continued high growth, but also near-term profitability pressure, since EBITDA is expected to be roughly breakeven.

Path to profitability

So this earnings was definitely an inflexion point. We’re seeing the high growth now get complemented by some emerging profitability and good forward visibility with that backlog. The numbers also indicate that the core business is beginning to scale efficiently. As per management, this whole progress journey is being captured in its achievement of the Rule of 40 in 4Q26. Now, this is a key SaaS metric where revenue growth plus EBITDA margin should be greater than or equal to 40%, which then indicates a healthy balance between growth and profitability. So on a full-year basis, Planet managed to reach a Rule of 30 a lot earlier than expected. Below is from CEO William Marshall.

“Q4 was also a record for revenue, representing 41% year-over-year growth and our fifth consecutive quarter of adjusted EBITDA profitability. For the second sequential quarter, we achieved the Rule of 40, which is revenue growth plus adjusted EBITDA margin. And on an annual basis, we achieved a Rule of 30 in a full year earlier than we anticipated. End-of-period backlog was over $900 million, approximately 79% growth year-on-year, providing us with excellent visibility to accelerate our revenue growth for the coming fiscal year.”

At the same time, the company’s forward visibility is what the market is not so bullish on. It strengthened dramatically, driven mainly by a surge in contracted demand. That backlog exceeded $900 million, up about 79%, and their remaining performance obligations hit $852 million, up about 106%. And keep in mind that about 65% expected to convert into revenue within 24 months, which is what we heard from CFO Ashley Johnson on the call, as you can see below.

“Our focus remains on managing the business to enable sustainable cash flow generation through efficient growth across our data solutions and Satellite Services revenue streams. At the end of FY '26, our Remaining Performance Obligations, or RPOs were approximately $852.4 million, up about 106% year-over-year, of which approximately 34% apply to the next 12 months and 65% to the next 24 months.”

The other good news is that the backlog expansion is purely sovereign satellite services deals, but also some growth in data and AI-driven solutions. The CFO also mentioned on the call that a lot of their "backlog growth is in data and solutions. In fact, that part of the backlog has almost doubled year-over-year."

Now, that's pretty significant because these offerings tend to be a bit higher-margin but also recurring since once a client gets a taste of the AI services and integrates them, it can be pretty hard to switch. It’s also far more scalable since Planet integrates AI capabilities that specifically allow customers to extract insights from the imagery and then deploy use cases faster. So that should be driving both expansion within their existing accounts and also new adoption.

However, the slight downside was that despite the strong visibility, management also embedded some degree of conservatism in the FY27 guidance. I think we can put this down to the lumpy and timing-sensitive nature of the larger government and satellite services contracts. This has actually been a recurring theme for a lot of small-cap defence players, where revenue recognition depends on milestone delivery and procurement schedules. So as a result, the timing of large new deal signings can materially impact results. Therefore, it means that Planet is assuming some deals will close later in the year, which then leaves room for upside if execution or contract timing comes in earlier than expected.

Valuation extremely stretched

When it comes to the valuation, the market is clearly pricing in some hefty future growth here. And what hasn't helped was the big 25% run-up post earnings. Now, despite generating $307.7 million in FY26 revenue, the company still trades at extremely elevated multiples with a 33.8x trailing sales and 26.9x forward sales multiple. Both of which are far above the sector median of roughly 2x. Likewise, the enterprise value to sales is sitting at 36.9x trailing and 26.5x forward, again going to show just how aggressively the stock is valued relative to its current scale.

I think the market seems to be underwriting a long-term thesis here with the focus mainly on high-margin, recurring geospatial data and analytics at scale. They hold an $11.5 billion market cap and pretty decent backlog visibility, so investors are essentially pricing in sustained 20% to 30% revenue growth with significant operating leverage going forward. This would also align with their remaining performance obligations of about $852.4 million, being up over 100% year-over-year, of which we can expect 34% to apply to the next 12 months and 65% to the next 24 months, as I mentioned earlier. Investors are also expecting a transition toward profitability over time. In that sense, the premium here would suggest expectations that Planet can evolve into a category-defining data platform, and I have to agree with them. It could be potentially akin to a space-based SaaS model, in which we see margins expand meaningfully as their satellite infrastructure scales. AI-driven insights would be another differentiating factor to increase monetisation. However, this also leaves very little room for execution missteps, and as the current multiple already embeds a very optimistic view of future growth, buying in at the current levels in the mid-$33 range may not be the best. In recent times, this has been quite volatile, so keep that in mind before buying in for long-term upside.

Just for a quick peer group comparison, Planet’s valuation is even pretty extreme among space-based SaaS peers. For example, BlackSky Technology Inc. (BKSY) trades at a forward EV to sales of 7.98x and a forward price to sales of 7.34x, compared with Planet’s 26.5x forward EV to sales. So roughly a 3x premium. Both are beginning to operate subscription-like satellite data models with the integration of AI for decision-making, but I feel Planet’s larger scale and multi-year government contracts are driving the market to price in a potential category-defining platform, similar to a space-based SaaS analogy here.

Key risks to keep in mind

So, beyond just the high valuation concerns, execution risk is going to come into play here. Planet now needs to consistently convert its $900 million backlog into revenue on time, sustain 20% to 30% growth, and also successfully scale/integrate those higher-margin AI analytics. It's especially important given that Planet relies on large, complex government and sovereign contracts. As you know, these deals are often milestone-based and subject to lengthy procurement cycles. They can also be slightly vulnerable to political changes. Even if we see minor delays or timing mismatches, revenue recognition could get a little lumpy. Now, usually this wouldn't be too much of a concern with a growing company, but due to the market’s high expectations for consistent growth here, it could have an impact on investor sentiment. This is especially true for a stock now priced for near-flawless execution; this kind of variability could possibly trigger outsized downside reactions.

Furthermore, to touch on profitability again, even though the company has recently shown us those early signs, margins remain thin and not that firmly established yet. So in that sense, there is little cushion if revenue growth moderates. Any deceleration here, whether due to macroeconomic pressure, customer budget constraints, or just a slower-than-expected deal conversion, could lead to multiple compressions, again given the stock’s premium valuation. Overall, the business is very capital-intensive. We're seeing a significant ongoing investment to expand satellite infrastructure and continue advancing AI capabilities. And whilst these investments are intended to support their long-term scalability and margin expansion, they also inevitably introduce some degree of near-term financial pressure. So adoption of these technologies has to be strong going forward and not lag expectations.

Finally, just from a technical and sentiment perspective, the stock has had that significant run-up, up about 162% since initial coverage and an additional 25% following 4Q26 earnings, which of course raises the risk of profit-taking.

Looking ahead

I think the main question we all have after these earnings is whether Planet can actually justify a valuation that already prices in major success. It should ultimately come down to execution against a very demanding set of expectations from the market, even more so after the run-up. On one hand, the bull case would argue that the current valuation, even though it’s appearing extreme at 27x forward sales and 37x trailing EV to sales on about $308M in FY26 revenue, is essentially underwriting a future where Planet scales into a multi-billion-dollar, high-margin data platform. That would also make up much of the long-term buy case here and leans pretty heavily on the $900 million backlog and guidance for $415 million to $440 million revenue in FY27. So about 39% growth, which would suggest sustained demand, particularly from defence and sovereign clients. Now, if we do see Planet maintain growth of 20% to 30% and expand margins via their software/AI monetisation innovations, today’s valuation could compress naturally as the fundamentals catch up. Particularly given that profitability seems to be just around the corner.

On the other hand, the bear case seems to be bringing us back to reality in that the market is already pricing in much of this success. Even if we use forward estimates, the company trades at a hefty premium to peers, while EBITDA is only expected to be $0 to $10 million in FY27. So would that then imply minimal near-term profitability? What that does is create a narrow margin for error, in that any slowdown in growth or delays in large government contracts, like we just saw, could trigger significant multiple compression. This is especially true when you add in the natural volatility of a small defence player. If you remember, back in January, this stock was nearing the $30 mark before pulling back to $20.

Furthermore, the backlog is still only about 2x to 3x annual revenue, so the company must continuously replenish these large deals to sustain the trajectory. So essentially, the stock reflects a belief that Planet will successfully transition from a capital-intensive satellite operator into a scalable, high-margin data/AI platform, but execution doesn’t just need to be good; it needs to be consistently strong over multiple years to validate the current price. However, I remain buy-rated on Planet with a longer outlook, since I see it evolving more into a category-defining space-based data and AI platform, with that upside coming from sustained 20% to 30% growth and, importantly, expanding margins. In that sense, I feel there's further long-term upside despite the near-term volatility we may see.

data.title: KE Holdings: Staying Neutral As I Await The Core Business To Rejuvenate
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about KE Holdings Inc. (BEKE) previously, giving it a hold rating as the operating backdrop (the property market) was still in a downturn. The key operating metric, transaction volumes, continues to decline as well. Looking at the recent results, I still do not see enough evidence that BEKE can get back to a more normal growth path anytime soon, and management’s own neutral 2026 market view only reinforces my point. I stay hold rated.

4Q25 earnings

BEKE reported a horrible set of Q4 numbers. Net revenues came in at RMB22.2 billion, down 28.7% y/y from RMB31.1 billion, dragged down by a total GTV decline of 36.7% y/y. Of which, existing home transaction services revenue fell 39% y/y, and within that, commission revenue fell 42.9% to RMB4.2 billion, while platform service, franchise service, and other value-added services revenue fell 19.9%. New home transaction services revenue also fell 44.5% y/y. Even revenue from home renovation and furnishing fell 12% y/y. The only part of the business that did really well was home rental services, which saw revenue grow by 18.1% y/y. As you can expect, margins and profits were down as well. Gross profit fell 33.7% y/y, with gross margin down to 21.4% (down 160 bps y/y), and combined with a step-up in operating cost, BEKE reported an operating loss of RMB147 million. Note that Q4 2024 did EBIT of ~RMB1 billion. In just a year, the entire profit pool of ~RMB1 billion got wiped out. In terms of margin, EBIT margin came in at -0.7%, versus 3.2% last year.

The core business is still going the wrong way

I continue to stay on the sidelines for BEKE because the two businesses (existing home and new home) that still matter most to BEKE’s earnings power had a bad quarter, and I saw no real signs that the pressure is ending. To be fair, Q4 2024 was a high comp base, so the like-for-like decline may be less than the reported headline numbers. But still, it does not change the fact that business continues to slow, and the worrisome part is that this is a broad-based decline. It was not due to one line item or one channel. To reiterate, in existing homes, commission revenue fell 42.9%, and even the platform, franchise, and other value-added revenue that had been more resilient also fell 19.9%. In the new home segment, the drop was even worse. GTV was down 41.7%, and revenue was down 44.5%. One slight positive point here is that the new home contribution margin improved to 28.3% due to a better cost structure, but this is just not enough to move the needle when GTV is down >40% y/y.

I believe these two segments are all that matter for the stock to re-rate in the near term. BEKE needs to convince investors that it can get back to a more normal growth path once the operating backdrop gets better, and clearly, Q4 did not give enough evidence for that. I would also point out that management itself is not confident of a V-shaped recovery as well. In the call, they articulated their position to maintain a neutral market view for 2026. Reuters had a good piece on China’s property market, which is aligned with management’s view, and I urge readers to take a look. In any case, the setup is not great. The reported numbers show no signs of demand recovering, management is not guiding for a recovery either, and third-party research shows 2027 is the year of “stabilization.” Therefore, I do not see much basis to upgrade BEKE to a buy rating anytime soon.

Some positive points

Now, not everything is bad. BEKE is doing some things better, and the diversification element of this equity story is gaining more foothold. Management highlighted that non-housing transaction businesses now represent 41% of total revenue in 2025. If we combine this with the fact that the core business is slowing down, it implies that the newer businesses are growing significantly. I think Rental is the best example of what is working. Revenue grew 18.1% y/y, and units under management went up ~62% y/y to >700,000. Contribution margin also improved to 10.4% in Q4. I get that rental is still a lower-margin business that is still scaling. If we look further out, say 5 years, when the Chinese property market has hopefully recovered, the BEKE business would be much more resilient to the general property cycle, which means through-cycle valuation multiples should be relatively higher.

There are also the AI and decision-support angles, which I think are two of the more interesting new parts of the story. As a background recap, BEKE is not using AI as some separate product that creates a new revenue stream; it is trying to use AI inside the workflow to improve matching, pricing support, agent productivity, and conversion. I think this is a much better use of AI than a new product because BEKE is still a very labour-intensive business with a lot of offline presence. The value of AI here is not to roll out new flashy features but to help in reducing friction at each step of the manpower process. If BEKE can use AI to deliver better pricing support, the rate of clearing listings should step up; better matching should also help improve conversion (right buyer for the right property); and better workflow support should let each person handle more without costs increasing at the same pace. If this works, I expect the results to not just be better service but also better unit economics over time.

With that said, I would still urge investors to stay in the cautious camp for now because management itself said 2026 will be the year it tests how this model improves conversion rates and unit economics. So, everything is on the table. This entire AI strategy could fail to take off as labor on the ground refuses to learn and adapt. Or, it could work very well, and things could play out as I expected above. As of today, the strategy seems sound, but we still lack tangible proof that it works.

Valuation

As expected, BEKE’s stock remains rangebound at the ~20x forward P/E range, now at ~19.5x. The developments so far are still fairly mixed. On one hand, you have tangible improvements seen in diversification, especially in rental, and also that promising AI strategy that could improve unit economics. But all of these don’t matter if new and existing homes remain under pressure, and management themselves have kept a neutral 2026 market view. Besides, it is also hard to imagine BEKE trading any higher from here when Zillow Group, Inc. (Z), which is expected to grow revenue at >10% for the next two years, trades just at 21x NTM P/E. Unless one has extreme confidence in the China property turning around, so much that BEKE’s growth could exceed Z’s by a big margin (BEKE’s current expected revenue CAGR is mid-single-digits for the next 2 years), I really doubt we will see any multiple expansion here.

Conclusion

I reiterate my hold rating for BEKE. I believe the business is becoming more diversified and potentially more resilient over time. Rental is scaling well, and the AI and decision-support strategy could eventually improve unit economics. The problem is that none of this is enough yet to offset the continued weakness in the existing home segment and the new home segment, which I believe still drives the near-term debate around the stock. Until those core segments show clearer signs of stabilizing, I do not see a strong reason to turn more bullish.

data.title: 3 Strong International Equity Dividend ETFs
data.description:
International equities trade at discounted valuations relative to comparable U.S. equities. Investing in these securities also increases portfolio diversification, at least for investors focusing on U.S. securities. International equity ETFs provide investors with simple, effective ways to gain exposure to international equities. In this article, I'll be giving a quick rundown of three particularly strong ETFs in this area. These are:

The Amplify CWP International Enhanced Div Inc ETF (IDVO) is an active ETF investing in fifty international equities and writing covered calls on a portion of its holdings. IDVO is an incredibly well-rounded investment, with a solid 5.6% dividend yield, strong dividend growth, and a performance track record. Lots of benefits, fewer downsides.

The State Street SPDR EURO STOXX 50 ETF (FEZ) is an index ETF investing in 50 of the largest European equities in the market. FEZ has the lowest 3.0% dividend yield of the bunch but the highest dividend growth and strongest performance since inception. Solid choice and should be of particular interest to investors looking for quality or wishing to focus on European markets.

The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF (SCHY) is an index ETF investing in 100 international equities, based on several dividend and quality factors. Simplest, most vanilla choice of the three, with a 3.4% dividend yield, but the weakest returns since inception.

In my opinion, the three ETFs above are all solid investment opportunities and buys. IDVO would be my top pick, as it's the most well-rounded investment, but all three have their merits, and covered call ETFs are not for everyone and have their downsides.

Quick International Equities Investment Thesis

A quick look at the investment thesis for international equities, before looking into the ETFs themselves.

The international equities investment thesis rests on their cheap valuations and the diversification these provide to U.S. investors.

Valuations are pretty self-explanatory, with most markets trading with discounted prices relative to U.S. equities, at least on an earnings basis:

The same is true when comparing benchmark U.S. equity and international equity index ETFs:

International equity prices have significantly declined over the past few weeks following the ongoing trouble in Iran and the Strait of Hormuz. Seems like a good time to buy, assuming conditions don't immediately deteriorate.

International equities provide some measure of diversification to U.S. investors, too. Specifically, as these are somewhat different types of investments, sometimes international equities outperform, sometimes significantly so. International equities are down 0.9% for the year, compared to S&P 500 losses of 4.6%. Sizable spread, and the difference was much more significant a month or two ago.

As should be clear from the above, investors with international equity exposure should have seen lower losses than those entirely focused on U.S. equities. Although U.S. equities aren't guaranteed to see any period of losses or underperformance moving forward, these do sometimes occur, and investing in international equities should help investors to weather these more easily.

So, investing in international equities means greater diversification and a cheaper portfolio, which could result in both higher returns and lower risk. Let's now take a look at some better international equity ETFs in the market.

IDVO: Covered Call ETF, Well-Rounded Choice

Strategy and Portfolio

IDVO is an actively managed international equity covered call ETF. It invests in around 50 companies selected in the following manner:

Largest holdings are as follows:

IDVO focuses on developed markets and leans toward old-economy industries like financials and industrials.

IDVO's covered call strategy reduces upside potential but boosts the fund's distribution yield to 5.6%. Dividends should grow too because of underlying equity distribution growth and due to asset growth, leading to more covered calls being sold. Dividends have seen strong double-digit growth since inception, in late 2022, averaging around 13.5% per year.

On the flipside, said strategy also moderately reduces upside potential, especially during more aggressive bull markets. Capital gains seem likely, definitely possible, although I would expect below-average gains long-term. Depending on the magnitude of these, the fund's dividends might result in outperformance on a total return basis regardless.

IDVO has significantly outperformed U.S. and international equity indexes since inception. Outstanding results considering the fact that equity prices have risen quite aggressively these past few years, and the fund's covered call strategy limits any gains here. Some combination of alpha, distributions, favorable timing, and luck explains these results.

IDVO's performance is between that of SCHY and FEZ, with broadly bullish results because of the fund's covered calls.

Key Differentiators

IDVO's key differentiators are its well-rounded investment thesis and strong, albeit short, performance track record.

IDVO is an incredibly well-rounded ETF, with an incredibly broad set of advantages: it has an above-average 5.6% yield, distribution growth is strong, and it has outperformed the market since inception. IDVO's distributions could even lead to outperformance during bear markets, reducing drawdowns and risk. Lots of ETFs might be better than IDVO in one of these metrics; very few can match the fund in all of these.

IDVO's performance track record is pretty self-explanatory, with the fund outperforming relevant equity indexes since inception:

and for most time periods:

IDVO's +20% in annualized returns is, of course, outstanding. Outperforming its index by over 3% a year is great too. Outperforming during a period of significant equity market gains is incredibly surprising because the fund's covered call strategy limits upside potential. As mentioned previously, some combination of alpha, distributions, favorable timing, and luck explains these results.

I last covered IDVO here.

FEZ: European Equities Index ETF

Strategy and Portfolio

FEZ is a simple European equities index ETF. It invests in the largest European companies within each sector, subject to a basic set of inclusion criteria, totaling 50 stocks in total. The largest of these are as follows:

FEZ leans toward old-economy value sectors, including financials and industrials, being somewhat underweight tech in turn. It is also underweight on energy and materials, as Europe simply has less of these natural resources compared to the U.S. or emerging markets.

FEZ invests in several countries but is significantly overweight in France and Germany due to the comparatively greater size of their economies and public equity markets. France is the largest position at a 32.7% weight, surpassing Germany due to differences in how their companies are (generally) structured: public companies are more common in France, and there are lots of private medium-sized companies in Germany.

FEZ's 3.0% dividend yield is quite a bit higher than the S&P 500's 1.1% figure, due to the fund focusing on European (so international) equities. FEZ's dividend yield is slightly lower than the 2.9% international equity average, as European companies are generally of above-average quality, so they sport slightly above-average valuations and prices.

Although FEZ does not specifically target or focus on companies with above-average dividend growth, the fund's dividends have seen solid growth since inception, in the double digits these past five years. Favorable currency price movements explain some of these results, so it's difficult to ascertain their significance. Obviously, dividends should decline if the dollar appreciates in value, but I'm not sure how much growth to expect if currency prices are stable.

FEZ's performance track record seems reasonable enough, with the fund outperforming international equities since inception, significantly so these past five years. It has underperformed relative to the S&P 500 for the same reason, especially since the Iranian bombings, presumably due to bearish sentiment/expectations on European stocks due to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

Key Differentiator

FEZ's key differentiator is the fact that it exclusively invests in European equities, with IDVO and SCHY both being more regionally diversified. FEZ's more targeted portfolio is of higher quality but less diversified too. Focusing on Europe could lead to underperformance due to specific regional issues or global issues with a greater impact on Europe. The closure of the Strait of Hormuz was one such issue, and others could arise. Potential gains are arguably a bit lower, too, as riskier emerging markets could always see higher gains than calmer European markets. FEZ has outperformed broader international equity indexes since inception; see above.

I last covered FEZ here.

SCHY: Simple International Dividend Equity Index ETF

Strategy and Portfolio

SCHY is a simple international dividend equity index ETF. It invests in 100 equities, focusing on those with particularly good yields, dividend growth track records, ROE, and free cash flow to debt. The largest of these are as follows:

As is the case with most dividend ETFs, it is overweight several old-economy industries like financials and industrials and underweight tech. The same is true of IDVO, but the tilts are much more aggressive for SCHY.

Country weights are as follows:

SCHY focuses on stocks with good dividend yields, among other factors, which should boost yields somewhat. SCHY's 3.4% dividend yield is quite a bit higher than the S&P 500's 1.1% figure, but most of that is due to the fund's international equity focus, with index ETFs tracking these securities yielding around 2.9%. SCHY's index should boost dividend yield somewhat, and that is indeed the case, but that somewhat isn't all that high in practice.

Dividend growth is quite strong too, averaging 8.2% per year over the past three years. Dividends have been down these past twelve months, but that seems to be due to normal ETF dividend volatility.

SCHY compares unfavorably to IDVO on both dividend yield and dividend growth, but the fund's potential capital gains are higher, as these are not weighted down by any sort of covered call strategy.

SCHY's performance seems adequate, with the fund outperforming international equity indexes since inception, underperforming the S&P 500. Performance has improved these past few years but significantly worsened these past few months.

Key Differentiator

SCHY is a simple international dividend equity index ETF and so does not significantly differ from these securities as an asset class. As a result, the fund might be of particular interest to investors wishing for simple exposure to these securities without the added baggage of active management, covered calls, a focus on a specific region, etc.

I last covered SCHY here.

Conclusion

International equities trade at discounted valuations relative to U.S. equities and have some diversification benefits. The international equity ETFs presented in this article all share these benefits, are strong investment opportunities, and are buys.

data.title: Business Conditions Monthly January 2026
data.description:
By Peter C. Earle

Consumer-level price pressures have eased, but upstream costs are building. Rising producer prices and higher input costs — particularly in metals and energy — suggest continued pipeline pressures.

Note: As of the January 2026 Business Conditions Monthly (BCM) calculation, sufficient data have become available to resume publication of the three BCM diffusion indices. However, because the most recent fully complete run of constituent data dates back to July 2025, there is a discontinuity in the series. As a result, the current readings should be interpreted on a standalone basis — reflecting present conditions — rather than as the latest observation in a continuous trend. Until several additional months of consistent data are released and any revisions are incorporated, caution is warranted in drawing conclusions about momentum or directional changes in underlying economic conditions.

The Leading Indicator stands at 63, indicating a modestly positive tilt in forward-looking measures, with pockets of resilience in expectations-sensitive components. The Roughly Coincident Indicator registers at 42, suggesting somewhat subdued but not contracting real-time activity, consistent with a mixed and uneven current economic environment. Meanwhile, the Lagging Indicator comes in at 33, pointing to relatively soft backward-looking conditions, particularly in areas tied to credit and price dynamics.

Leading Indicator (63)

The Leading Indicator registered 63, with seven of 12 components improving, one unchanged, and four declining.

Gains were led by market-sensitive and forward-looking components. The University of Michigan Consumer Expectations Index rose 7.1 percent, while the Conference Board US Leading Index Stock Prices 500 Common Stocks increased 1.7 percent. The Conference Board US Manufacturers New Orders Nondefense Capital Goods Ex Aircraft advanced 0.5 percent, and US New Privately Owned Housing Units Started by Structure Total SAAR climbed 4.8 percent. United States Heavy Truck Sales SAAR surged 16.7 percent, and Debit Balances rose 0.9 percent. Labor market forward conditions improved as US Initial Jobless Claims SA declined 6.0 percent (a positive after inversion). Offsetting these gains, the Inventory-to-Sales Ratio Total Business fell 0.7 percent, US Average Weekly Hours All Employees Manufacturing SA declined 0.2 percent, and the Conference Board US Leading Index Manufacturers’ New Orders Consumer Goods and Materials slipped 0.3 percent. The 1-to-10 Year Treasury Yield Spread widened sharply by 70.2 percent but was scored negatively given its inversion. Adjusted Retail and Food Services Sales Total SA was effectively unchanged. Overall, the leading profile reflects strength in expectations, housing, and financial conditions, partially offset by softness in production-related metrics and the yield curve signal.

Roughly Coincident Indicator (42)

The Roughly Coincident Indicator came in at 42, with two components improving, one unchanged, and three declining.

On the positive side, Conference Board Coincident Manufacturing and Trade Sales increased 0.8 percent, and US Industrial Production rose 0.3 percent. However, Conference Board Coincident Personal Income Less Transfer Payments declined 0.2 percent, and Conference Board Consumer Confidence Present Situation SA fell 2.1 percent. US Labor Force Participation Rate edged down 0.2 percent, while US Employees on Nonfarm Payrolls Total SA was essentially flat. The balance of evidence suggests modest activity in production and sales, but weakening income growth, participation, and sentiment are weighing on current conditions.

Lagging Indicator (33)

The Lagging Indicator stood at 33, with two components improving and four declining.

US CPI Urban Consumers Less Food and Energy Year over Year NSA rose 0.2 percent, and US Manufacturing and Trade Inventories Total SA increased 0.1 percent. In contrast, US Commercial Paper Placed Top 30 Day Yield declined 5.3 percent, Conference Board US Lagging Commercial and Industrial Loans fell 0.7 percent, and Census Bureau US Private Construction Spending Nonresidential NSA dropped 0.7 percent. The Conference Board US Lagging Average Duration of Unemployment rose 5.6 percent and was scored negatively after inversion. Taken together, the lagging profile points to persistent inflation alongside softening credit conditions, declining construction activity, and lengthening unemployment duration — signals consistent with a cooling economic backdrop.

The January 2026 BCM readings suggest an economy with a modestly positive forward tilt, uneven current activity, and still-soft trailing conditions, but with an important caveat given the break in the data. The Leading Indicator (63) points to resilience in expectations, housing, and market-sensitive components, indicating some forward momentum despite lingering weaknesses in production metrics and an adverse yield curve signal. In contrast, the Roughly Coincident Indicator (42) reflects a mixed present, where gains in production and sales are offset by declining income growth, sliding labor force participation, and soft consumer sentiment, leaving real-time activity subdued. The Lagging Indicator (33) reinforces a softer backdrop, with tightening credit conditions, weakening construction, and longer unemployment durations outweighing persistent inflation pressures. Taken as a whole, the configuration is consistent with tentative forward strength layered over fragile current conditions and weakening underlying fundamentals. But because these figures follow a several-month gap in complete data, they should be interpreted cautiously as a snapshot of current conditions rather than as evidence of a sustained trend or turning point.

Discussion, February — March 2026

The latest inflation data present a conflicted but still informative picture, with early signs of disinflation at the consumer level offset by mounting upstream price pressures and a firmer underlying trend in services. February’s Consumer Price Index (CPI) came in softer than typical seasonal patterns would suggest, with headline inflation rising 0.27 percent month-over-month and holding at 2.4 percent year-over-year, while core CPI moderated to 0.22 percent. Much of the cooling reflects easing in heavily weighted categories such as rents and vehicles, and a broad decline in the share of items experiencing rapid price increases. Pockets of firmness remain, however, including apparel, discretionary services, and select goods categories influenced by rising input costs — particularly metals and memory chips — hinting at emerging supply-side pressures. At the same time, January’s Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) data showed a hotter underlying inflation trend, with core PCE rising 0.36 percent on the month and 3.1 percent year-over-year, driven largely by health care and other service categories. Consumer spending continues to rotate away from goods and toward services, even as income growth remains modest and the saving rate rises to 4.5 percent, suggesting cautious but still resilient household behavior.

Upstream, February’s Producer Price Index (PPI) reinforces the notion that cost pressures are building beneath the surface. Headline PPI rose a stronger-than-expected 0.7 percent on the month and accelerated to 3.4 percent year-over-year, reflecting higher transportation, warehousing, and especially metals costs — now up more than 15 percent since tariff measures took effect last year. While some components feeding into core PCE, such as health care and airfares, may exert less upward pressure in the near term, the broader trend in input costs points to continued pipeline inflation. Overlaying this dynamic is the emerging energy shock tied to the Iran conflict, which is likely to push headline CPI higher in the near term, potentially toward 3 percent, complicating the Federal Reserve’s policy path. Taken together, the data suggest that while consumer-level inflation has shown tentative signs of cooling, underlying service-sector strength and rising input costs — combined with geopolitical risks — leave the inflation outlook uncertain and increasingly sensitive to both supply shocks and policy responses.

Recent US labor market data point to a clear cooling in hiring, though the signal is muddied by temporary disruptions and measurement issues. February nonfarm payrolls fell by 92,000 — well below expectations — and the three-month average slowed to just 5,700 jobs, indicating that hiring momentum has nearly stalled and is likely running below breakeven. Some of the weakness reflects one-off factors, including a major health-care strike, adverse winter weather, and payback from unusually strong January conditions, while revisions to business formation estimates may have further amplified the decline. Even so, broader indicators suggest genuine softening: the unemployment rate rose to 4.44 percent, driven by job losers, while rising inflows into unemployment and declining outflows point to a less dynamic labor market. Wage growth remains relatively firm at 0.4 percent, but appears uneven and partly distorted by sector-specific effects, and aggregate income growth has softened as declining payrolls offset earnings gains.

At the same time, complementary data suggest the labor market is not collapsing but instead settling into a looser equilibrium. Job openings rose unexpectedly to 6.95 million in January, and private payroll data showed a modest gain of 63,000 jobs in February, with hiring concentrated in health care, education, and smaller firms. However, the ratio of vacancies to unemployed workers remains below one, indicating that slack persists and that labor demand is no longer outpacing supply. The quits rate has stabilized and layoffs have edged lower, reinforcing the view of reduced churn rather than renewed strength. Taken together, the data describe a labor market that is cooling and stabilizing at a lower level of activity — no longer a clear source of inflationary pressure, but not yet signaling a sharp deterioration — leaving policymakers inclined toward caution but still biased toward eventual easing.

The February ISM surveys paint a picture of continued expansion across both manufacturing and services, though with important differences in momentum and inflation signals. The ISM Manufacturing PMI edged down slightly to 52.4 but came in above expectations, indicating ongoing — if somewhat moderating — growth in the industrial sector. Demand remains solid, with new orders still firmly in expansion territory and supported by low customer inventories and improving backlogs, suggesting continued production ahead. The headline was bolstered by gains in employment, inventories, and slower supplier deliveries, the latter signaling capacity strain. However, a sharp rise in the prices-paid index — driven in part by higher metals costs and tariff-related pressures — points to renewed input cost inflation that may concern policymakers even as output stabilizes.

In contrast, the ISM Services PMI delivered a stronger and more broadly positive signal, jumping to 56.1, its highest level since mid-2022. The increase was driven by a surge in new orders, accelerating production, and improving employment, indicating robust demand across the dominant services sector. Backlogs and export orders also strengthened, while supply chains showed modest improvement. Importantly, price pressures became less pervasive, with the services prices index declining, offering some relief on the inflation front. Taken together, the two reports suggest a US economy entering the year with improving activity and sentiment, led by services strength and supported by steady manufacturing demand, but with a divergence in inflation dynamics — easing in services while intensifying in goods — that complicates the broader outlook.

Recent sentiment data across consumers and small businesses point to a cautiously stable but increasingly fragile outlook, with geopolitical and energy developments emerging as key risks. The University of Michigan’s preliminary consumer sentiment index slipped modestly to 55.5 in March, as a decline in expectations more than offset a slight improvement in current conditions, with the drop largely concentrated after the onset of military action in Iran. Inflation expectations remained relatively anchored — unchanged at 3.4 percent for one year and easing slightly to 3.2 percent over five years — but rising gasoline prices, up roughly 27 percent this month to their highest levels since late 2023, pose a growing threat to real incomes and future confidence. Small-business sentiment remains just above its long-run average, with the NFIB index at 98.8, supported by improved recent sales and profit trends, but forward-looking indicators have softened, including a notable drop in expected sales and only modest plans for capital spending. While fewer firms are currently raising prices, a significant share still intends to do so, reflecting persistent cost pressures. Across businesses, taxes remain the top concern, followed by labor quality, inflation, and weakening sales, with rising competition and financing concerns also in the mix. Taken together, the data suggest that while sentiment has not yet deteriorated sharply, it is increasingly vulnerable to energy-driven cost shocks and geopolitical uncertainty, which could weigh on both consumer demand and business confidence in the months ahead.

Retail sales data and the latest consumer surveys together suggest a consumption picture that is shifting from temporary softness toward more structural pressure. Retail sales dipped 0.2 percent in January, largely due to winter weather disruptions that curtailed in-person activity, especially dining out, while boosting online purchases, which helped cushion the decline. Core measures excluding autos and gasoline remained modestly positive, indicating that underlying demand had not collapsed and could rebound as weather effects fade. However, that near-term stabilization is now being challenged by a sharp energy-driven shock tied to the Iran conflict, with gasoline prices jumping from about $2.94 to $3.63 per gallon and oil rising roughly 40 percent. This surge has rapidly fed into consumer expectations, with anticipated gas price increases and broader inflation expectations moving higher, while perceptions of personal finances have deteriorated meaningfully.

More concerning for the consumption outlook is the breadth of this shift in sentiment. Higher-income households — previously a key driver of spending strength — have also pulled back sharply, raising the risk that aggregate consumption may weaken more broadly. At the same time, rising fuel costs are acting as a direct drag on discretionary spending, effectively reallocating household budgets toward necessities while amplifying price pressures in areas like transportation and travel. Coupled with growing concerns about job security and declining confidence in future income, these developments suggest that while early-year retail weakness may have been partly weather-related, the outlook for consumer spending is increasingly constrained by a combination of eroding purchasing power and heightened uncertainty.

Productivity and industrial output data together point to an economy that is still generating growth efficiently, even as sectoral performance remains uneven. Labor productivity rose at a solid 2.8 percent annualized pace in the fourth quarter, with prior quarters revised higher due to lower measured hours worked, reinforcing a favorable underlying trend. While unit labor costs increased at a 2.8 percent annualized rate in the quarter — driven by stronger compensation — the four-quarter trend remains subdued at just 1.3 percent, indicating that labor costs are not exerting meaningful inflationary pressure. This combination — steady productivity gains alongside contained cost growth — suggests that output can continue to expand without forcing a policy response from the Federal Reserve on labor-driven inflation grounds.

Industrial production data echo this theme of modest but uneven expansion. Output rose 0.2 percent in February, supported by gains in manufacturing — particularly transportation equipment and business investment-related categories — while consumer goods production was flat, with durable gains offset by declines in nondurables. Utilities dragged on the headline amid weak natural gas output, while mining and energy extraction showed early signs of strengthening, likely to become more important given geopolitical supply disruptions. Taken together, the data suggest a production environment characterized by resilience in capital-intensive and strategic sectors, softer performance in consumer-facing goods, and an overall growth path that remains intact but far from broad-based.

The early March Beige Book points to an economy that is losing some momentum but largely maintaining its footing, with activity expanding at a slight to moderate pace in seven of twelve Federal Reserve districts, down from eight previously, and a growing share reporting flat or declining conditions. Consumer behavior remains uneven, with heightened price sensitivity among lower-income households weighing on discretionary spending, including weaker auto sales. At the same time, manufacturing conditions improved, supported in part by demand tied to data centers and energy infrastructure, highlighting a divergence between capital-intensive sectors and more consumer-facing areas. Employment was broadly stable, though firms cited softer demand, rising input costs, and uncertainty as constraints on hiring. Price pressures remained moderate, with tariffs contributing to elevated costs across most districts, but firms expressed expectations for some easing ahead — an encouraging signal that is now complicated by renewed geopolitical risks. While business sentiment has become more optimistic, the report largely predates the escalation of the Iran conflict and related policy uncertainty, suggesting that current conditions may understate the degree of volatility and downside risk facing the economy in the near term.

The current policy mix reflects a growing tension between a still-restrictive monetary stance and a fiscal impulse that is increasingly being offset by external shocks. At its March meeting, the Federal Reserve held rates steady at 3.50 percent to 3.75 percent but signaled a more hawkish underlying posture, raising its estimates of long-run growth and the neutral rate — partly on expectations of AI-driven productivity gains — while also revising inflation forecasts higher. Although the median projection still includes one rate cut this year, the upward shift in the dot plot and higher assumed policy baseline suggest a reduced willingness to ease quickly, especially amid lingering inflation risks and geopolitical uncertainty. This leaves policy effectively tighter than it may appear on the surface, particularly as the Fed balances stronger projected growth against rising inflation pressures and an unsettled global backdrop.

At the same time, the anticipated boost from fiscal policy is being eroded in real time. The One Big Beautiful Bill Act was expected to support consumption through increased tax refunds — roughly $650 to $800 per household, contributing about 0.4 percentage points to GDP — but that impulse is now being offset by a sharp rise in energy prices tied to the Iran conflict. With oil prices already above the estimated breakeven level of roughly $83 per barrel, higher gasoline costs are effectively neutralizing the benefit of those refunds, particularly for lower-income households that spend a larger share of income on fuel. In effect, the policy mix is shifting from one of modest support to one of partial offset, where fiscal stimulus is diluted by energy-driven real income losses and monetary policy remains cautious. Together, this creates a more constrained near-term outlook, in which growth depends increasingly on higher-income consumers and favorable financial conditions, both of which are vulnerable to ongoing geopolitical and market volatility.

The US economy is showing modest but uneven growth, with services and productivity providing support while manufacturing, consumption, and hiring lose some momentum. Inflation appears to be cooling at the consumer level, but rising input costs and an energy shock tied to the Iran conflict are reintroducing upward pressure and complicating the outlook. The labor market is clearly softening, while consumer spending faces increasing strain from higher gasoline prices, weakening sentiment, and a potential pullback among higher-income households. Looking ahead, an increasingly cautious Federal Reserve (possibly facing a policy trap) and fiscal boost increasingly offset by energy costs leave the expansion intact but fragile, with growth more vulnerable to geopolitical risks and shifts in confidence.

PXH Strategy

Invesco RAFI Emerging Markets ETF (PXH) was launched on 09/27/2007 and tracks the RAFI Fundamental Select Emerging Markets 350 Index. PXH has 357 holdings, a 12-month trailing yield of 4%, a 30-day SEC yield of 2.21%, and an expense ratio of 0.47%. Distributions are paid quarterly. As described in the prospectus by Invesco:

The Underlying Index is designed to track the performance of approximately 350 commons stocks of the largest emerging markets companies from the constituents of the RAFI Global Equity Investable Universe.

The RAFI methodology selects and weights stocks based on a size definition involving four fundamental metrics: book value plus intangibles, adjusted cash flow, adjusted sales, and dividends plus buybacks. This fundamental size offers an alternative to cap-weighted indexes and generally has a tilt to the value factor. The portfolio turnover rate was 27% in the most recent fiscal year. I will use as a benchmark the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (EEM).

PXH is part of a series of Invesco ETFs tracking RAFI indexes, also including:

  • Invesco RAFI US 1000 ETF (PRF).
  • Invesco RAFI US 1500 Small-Mid ETF (PRFZ).
  • Invesco RAFI Strategic US ETF (IUS).
  • Invesco RAFI Developed Mkts ex-US ETF (PXF).
  • Invesco RAFI Developed Mkts ex-US SM ETF (PDN).

Portfolio

The heaviest country in the portfolio is China (36.2% of asset value), followed by Taiwan (22.2%), which points to significant geopolitical risk. Other countries are below 13%. Compared to EEM, PXH overweights China and Brazil and excludes South Korea (considered a developed country by S&P since 2001).

The presence of Taiwan among emerging markets is debatable: it surpassed South Korea in GDP per capita in 2025. Additionally, both countries are ahead of the E.U., the U.K., and Japan in GDP at purchasing power parity per capita.

The fund has significant exposure in financials (26.7%) and technology (18.5%). Compared to EEM, PXH greatly downplays technology and offers a more balanced sector breakdown.

Company-specific risk is moderate, with 29.1% of asset value in the top 10 holdings (listed in the next table) and 6.2% in the largest position, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM).

Takeaway

The Invesco RAFI Emerging Markets ETF holds about 350 emerging market stocks selected and weighted with a “fundamental size” methodology. PXH is diversified across sectors and holdings and shows attractive valuation metrics, but has significant geopolitical risks, with 58% of assets in China and Taiwan. PXH has marginally outperformed the benchmark EEM since inception and is on par with key competitors FNDE and DFEV since 2022. PXH is well-suited for investors seeking exposure to the value factor in emerging markets for long-term or tactical allocation. Position sizing must keep geopolitical risk at an acceptable level.
