data.title: Keep Kraft Heinz In Your Pantry, Not Your Portfolio
data.description:
Background

I have recently become interested in high-dividend packaged foods as I observed iconic companies, including The Kraft Heinz Company (KHC), near historic lows with cheap valuations and tempting forward yields over 5%.

I began with an overview of high-dividend packaged food companies based on a matrix of factors, including forward yield, payout ratio, value, growth, profitability, and financial strength. Note: Kraft recently reported atypical losses on substantial impairments of its assets; therefore, it was evaluated based on its normalized financial figures. With that caveat, Kraft looks like an excellent value, offering a high forward yield, and appears to be one of the most favorable prospects in the industry.

However, I did not rush out to invest any part of my hard-won retirement funds in Kraft or advise others to buy. Instead, I began by digging into Kraft's Seeking Alpha Quant ratings.

Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings

Seeking Alpha rates Kraft at A for valuation, but F for growth, with profitability, momentum, and revisions at B-plus, C-minus, and D, respectively.

I am most concerned with Kraft's F rating on growth. I suspect the company's A on valuation partly reflects degraded market sentiment and its dismal growth rather than outstanding value. Kraft's 1Yr revenue, normalized net income, price return, and price/sales are plotted below.

Kraft: 1Yr

Over the last year, revenue decreased by about 2%, while normalized net margin decreased by almost 12%, resulting in downward pressure on shares. Price return was a loss of 27% while total return with dividends was a loss of about 23%. 5-year results are similar.

Kraft: 5Yr

Over the last five years, revenue decreased by almost 6%, while normalized net margin decreased by almost 11%. Price return was a loss of 47%, while total return with dividends was a loss of about 30%.

Valuation ratios have also trended down; Kraft is currently valued at 1.04 times sales versus its own 5-year average ratio of 1.60. Beyond trailing price/sales, Kraft's A rating on valuation is based on a comprehensive set of trailing and forward metrics; in most cases, the current ratio is more favorable than both its own five-year average and the consumer staples sector median ratio.

However, given several years of declining revenues, decaying margins, and negative returns, Kraft's low-value ratios might still indicate some relative value but mostly exceptionally gloomy sentiment. Investor optimism is exhausted, and the market is braced for further disappointments around Kraft. On that negative note, now seems like a good time to discuss another painful disappointment.

The Impairments

In the second quarter of 2025, Kraft reported a net income loss of nearly $8.0 billion, driven by non-cash impairment losses of $9.3 billion. I'm nearly certain that most investors and analysts have not fully considered how consequential this massive impairment is beyond the balance sheet. Kraft's 2Q25 report describes the impairment of goodwill as follows:

During the second quarter of 2025, we concluded that the sustained decline in our share price and market capitalization was a triggering event requiring an interim goodwill impairment assessment for all reporting units. We performed an interim impairment test (“Q2 Impairment Test”) as of the last day of our second quarter, June 28, 2025, and utilized the discounted cash flow method under the income approach to estimate the fair value of our reporting units.

Essentially, the present value of goodwill, including brand reputation and customer loyalty, was adjusted down because these balance sheet assets were expected to generate less cash flow. Let's recall that many investors are banking on brand reputation and customer loyalty when they invest in Kraft and imagine their investment will generate returns.

Separately, the company described its approach to reevaluating its intangibles, including the brands Kraft, Velveeta, Lunchables, and Maxwell House:

We concluded that the sustained decline in our share price and market capitalization was a triggering event requiring an interim indefinite-lived intangible asset impairment assessment for our brands. As part of the Q2 Impairment Test, we utilized the multi-period excess earnings and relief from royalty method under the income approach to estimate the fair value of our indefinite-lived intangible assets.

KHC is telling investors that some of its iconic brands are likely to contribute lesser future cash flows, such that their present value is reduced. Lesser cash flows clearly imply lesser revenues, lesser earnings, lesser dividend safety, lesser value, and ultimately lesser returns. I might suggest we should pay attention when a company that has produced a net loss of about 30% over the last five years tells us to expect less.

Forward Growth and Profitability

Seeking Alpha's Quant scores Kraft's growth and profitability at F and B-plus, respectively, on a comprehensive set of trailing and forward metrics by comparison to the consumer staples sector median and Kraft's own five-year average values. Readers are encouraged to review that data.

This analysis will focus on two rigorous and well-defined metrics, total revenue and normalized EPS, including historical results and consensus forward estimates. Three years of historical results and consensus estimated revenue and normalized earnings per share are plotted below through YE27.

Over the last three years, YE revenue has decreased about 6% to $24.9B, while EPS has decreased about 12% to $2.07/share. Looking forward, 2026 revenue and EPS are expected to contract 1-2% versus 2025 results before mounting a minuscule recovery in 2027.

Price/Sales

Assuming share prices remain flat, price/sales is expected to remain well below Kraft's 5-year average ratio and just below the consumer staples sector median.

Price/Earnings

While considering normalized earnings, PE is expected to further decrease from 10.57 to just 10.03 by early 2028, indicating possible value when compared to the 5-year average and sector median ratios.

Overall, it appears as though expectations are very low for Kraft, with revenue and earnings expected to be nearly flat through 2027. I'm even willing to consider that the bar might be so low that Kraft could meet or even exceed expectations. We might cautiously imagine that investor sentiment could shift if Kraft delivers results in line with estimates over a quarter or two.

I have found earnings revisions to be one of the best early indicators of future performance and market sentiment.

Earnings Revisions

Seeking Alpha rates Kraft at D for earnings revisions based on the general downward trend regarding consensus analyst revenue and earnings estimates. Consensus normalized quarterly earnings are plotted below.

1Q26 earnings estimates have been revised down about 20% from $0.63 to $0.50 over the last six months; later quarters are also trending down. Consensus EPS estimates reflect ten downward revisions regarding 1Q26 earnings over the last three months. Revenue estimates are also trending down, with seven downward revisions over the last three months.

Kraft's negative earnings revision trend is likely partly responsible for and could further degrade investor sentiment. I do not expect valuation ratios to expand or share prices to rebound meaningfully until investors see some upward earnings revisions.

Investor Takeaways

Kraft's mid-February earnings call included the following careful warning from the company's new CEO, Steven Cahillane:

...market conditions have gotten noticeably more challenging since the decision was first made to separate the company last summer. Consumer sentiment has worsened, industry trends have softened, and there is increasing volatility in the geopolitical landscape.

Just two weeks later, the conflict in Iran redefined “increasing volatility in the geopolitical landscape” for not only investors but also the entire globe. A comprehensive discussion of that conflict is far beyond the scope of this analysis, but it has already upset the global economy. Energy and fertilizer prices have spiked, with countless impacts on a business like Kraft. We can expect commodity inputs to become more costly while consumers become more cautious and even less willing to pay higher prices. I expect intensified pressure on Kraft's falling sales and contracting margins.

Besides the low investor expectations demonstrated by Kraft's relatively depressed valuation ratios, I can't find any cause for optimism. I would not be surprised to see further downward revisions on 2026 earnings before KHC's quarterly report on April 29th. I recommend investors sell Kraft at current market prices.

data.title: Consumer Sentiment Is Near The Breaking Point
data.description:
One of the more intriguing things about a stock market that remains resilient after three years of large gains from 2023 to 2025 and an economy that is still chugging along despite myriad worries is that overall consumer sentiment is in the tank and has been for some time.

And while the AI Revolution may garner the lion's share of financial headlines, consumers still drive nearly 70% of economic activity in the United States. One of the reasons consumer spending has held up well despite lousy consumer sentiment is that the top 10% of American households by income account for roughly half of all U.S. consumer spending. And this cohort has been boosted substantially from the huge gains in stocks (where they hold seven of every eight shares) in the past three years and price appreciation from other asset classes. Particularly, home price appreciation in the couple of years during and following COVID-19.

This segment should continue to spend healthily as long as stocks and real estate do not experience significant declines and AI doesn't trigger large-scale job loss in high-income professions.

However, most of the rest of the consumer base was already struggling to start 2026. Roughly two-thirds of Americans were already living paycheck to paycheck heading into last year's holiday season. In the paragraphs below, I discuss three key areas that look likely to get worse and push consumer sentiment and confidence down even further. Obviously, this would be a growing headwind for the overall U.S. economy.

Inflation

Things were getting worse on the inflation front before the opening of new hostilities in the Middle East. The month-over-month rise of .7% in the February Producer Price Index or PPI, was more than twice expectations. CPI has been largely stuck in a holding pattern for months now and is solidly above the Fed's official 2% target.

The Strait of Hormuz is effectively closed, with less than 5% of the previous volume of transit moving through daily. A huge amount of global LNG supplies traverses this chokepoint, or did before this new conflict. More than 20 million barrels of oil also went through this critical passageway daily. The closure has triggered a huge rise in oil and natural gas prices.

In addition, a large percentage of other commodities, chemicals, and some metals flow through the Strait. These include urea, nitrogen, and phosphate for fertilizer as well as chemicals like methanol. This situation has pushed diesel prices north of $5 a gallon for the first time since 2022, and this will push up logistical and transport costs as trucks and trains almost exclusively use diesel. Fertilizer prices are also surging and could disrupt planting seasons. Both these factors will push up food inflation in the weeks and months ahead.

Job Market

The war in the Middle East and surging energy prices are the last things the U.S. job market needs right now. Job growth was anemic in 2025 (roughly 15,000 new jobs a month after recent revisions) and has turned negative here in 2026. And few things cause more individual consumer angst than worsening job prospects or growing fears about losing a current position.

In addition, there are fears of the job market being disrupted by improving AI capabilities. Block, Inc. (XYZ) triggered the new worries on this front by announcing it was laying off 40% of its entire workforce in late February due to AI. Oracle Corporation (ORCL) and Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) look set to implement large-scale reductions in forces to free up capital for their massive AI-related capex budgets. Now, HSBC Holdings plc (HSBC) may lay off up to 20,000 employees as it automates its back-office processes with AI.

And surging energy and transportation costs coupled with concerns around tariff policies and the unknown of how much longer the Strait of Hormuz will be effectively closed is a toxic combination for hiring managers deciding to increase payroll, especially with a notable slowdown in GDP growth in Q4.

Home Price Appreciation

The health of the housing sector is a key determinant of consumer sentiment. This makes perfect sense. Nearly two-thirds of American households own a house, and for most, it is their largest asset. When home prices are increasing, it buoys confidence for existing homeowners. Obviously, when average home prices start to fall, it does the opposite and can create negative equity for homes that were purchased recently.

And higher mortgage rates since the Fed started to tighten monetary policy aggressively in March 2022 have had a major impact on existing home sales. 2023-2025 saw the lowest level of annual existing home sales since 1995. Interest rates rose that year as well, and the population of the U.S. was 20% lower three decades ago.

Housing was finally getting a reprieve early this year as average 30-year mortgage rates finally moved under 6% for the first time since 2022. Unfortunately, the conflict in Iran has triggered a big upward move in 10- and 30-year Treasury yields, which in turn has pushed average mortgage rates above 6.2% and to their highest levels since December.

In addition, things were looking dire in the new home sale space before this new regional war in the Middle East. January new home sales dropped over 11% from the same period a year ago. The average new home sale price fell nearly 7%. New home inventory rose to nearly 10 months of current demand.

Giant homebuilder Lennar Corporation (LEN) is spending 14% of home sales revenue on incentives (free upgrades, help with closing costs, mortgage buy-downs, etc.). Lennar's average sale price of a new home is now back to 2017 levels. Gross margins have fallen to 15.2% in the company's last quarter from a peak of 26.2% in the boom years following COVID-19.

The average price of a new home sale in January was $400,500, $2,500 above that of an average existing home sale that month. And new homes come with new appliances, lower maintenance/insurance costs, smaller electricity bills, and new roofs and offer incentives (especially mortgage rate buy-downs) that existing homes can't match. Given this, it is difficult to see how average existing home sale prices don't move lower, even as property taxes and insurance continue to increase faster than inflation. Additional job losses, rising deaths in the Baby Boomer generation (which owns approximately 40% of all U.S. residential housing stock), and increasing FHA foreclosure rates as Covid-era programs were finally ended last summer could put even more existing home inventory on the market as well.

Conclusion

Given the increasingly dire outlook for most U.S. consumers and the likelihood that things will get worse before they get better, I continue to underweight sectors reliant on the non-top 10% of American households by income. These sectors include retail, auto manufacturers, furnishings, consumer discretionary, lodging, and hospitality.

I expect restaurant chains like Wendy’s, Papa John's, and Pizza Hut to continue to close significant numbers of underperforming stores in 2026. A report earlier this year projected that 9% of full-service restaurants were at risk of closure this year. Individual chapter 11 bankruptcies rose 11% in 2025, and corporate bankruptcies increased 14% to their highest levels since the aftermath of the Great Financial Crisis.

Unfortunately, I see bankruptcies rising further for both consumers and corporations here in 2026. Especially if the growing crisis across the private credit markets triggers a credit crunch, and these potential scenarios are simply not priced into the current market, in my opinion.

data.title: CIO Notebook: Fed Holds Steady As Inflation Fears Grow
data.description:
By Shannon L. Saccocia, CFA,

We remain constructive on global equities with a focus on quality and value; within fixed income, we believe dispersion is creating opportunities, and given our rate outlook, there may be potential advantages to adding duration as the Iran conflict response evolves.

As expected, the Federal Open Market Committee (“FOMC”) held the fed funds target rate steady at 3.50% to 3.75% following their March meeting. The lone dissent to the decision came from Stephen Miran, who once again called for a 25-basis point cut – his fifth straight dissent. There were few changes in the Fed statement, namely an acknowledgement that “the unemployment rate has been little changed in recent months” and a reference to the current conflict in the Middle East, with the FOMC stating “The implications of developments in the Middle East for the U.S. economy are uncertain.”

With that said, the emphasis should be on the potential for higher energy costs to impact the Fed’s views on the economy. Admittedly, the quarterly update of economic projections reflected an increase in core PCE from +2.5% in December to +2.7% but also showed an increase in growth expectations for 2026, from +2.3% to +2.4%, with unemployment still expected at 4.4%.

However, during the press conference, Fed Chair Jerome Powell clarified that in the short term energy prices alone would not translate to higher core inflation, only headline; instead, he cited continued stickiness as a result of the transmission of tariffs combined with the potential for energy price passthrough to end consumers as the rationale for higher core price estimates. In addition, Powell attributed the increase in growth expectations to a rise in productivity on the back of AI implementation, squaring the persistence of the low hiring dynamic (and stable unemployment rate) despite higher economic growth.

The dot plot, the result of the Fed’s updated calculus, showed little change from December, with the median official expecting one quarter-point cut in 2026 and another in 2027. Powell appeared to point to the importance of the concept of the median, however, stating that there were some shifts among the nineteen submissions in the direction of fewer cuts – a more hawkish point against the backdrop of the modestly dovish projections and statement.

The most surprising portion of the press conference came as Powell confirmed that he would not only remain on as “chairman pro-tem” should his successor remain unconfirmed at the end of his term, he would also continue at the Fed through the conclusion of the Department of Justice investigation and has not yet made a decision about serving through the remainder of his term (January 2028) as a Fed board member.

Markets, which had already moved on a hotter-than-expected February PPI release (+0.7% vs. +0.3% month-over-month), reacted poorly to Powell’s comments. U.S. equities moved lower, and Treasury yields moved higher, perhaps an indication that despite the unchanged dot plot, investors believe higher inflation – not a deteriorating labor market – will be the driver of Fed policy over the coming months. The uncertainty around the duration and knock-on effects of the escalation in the Middle East was apparent in Powell’s comments, but the undercurrent of tariffs as a continued source of pricing pressure is likely to create discomfort even for those who believe there is a near-term off-ramp for the U.S. in Iran.

We believe the Fed still has some room to move this year, despite the threat of short-term upward pressure on prices. We remain constructive on global equities, with a continued emphasis on active selection across geographies; we also continue to favor quality and value, while admitting that there are likely to be opportunities in AI-related issues over the next several months. Within fixed income, dispersion is creating opportunities, and given our rate outlook, there may be opportunities to add duration as the Middle East conflict response evolves. We encourage investors to review their current allocations and to rebalance as appropriate to long-term targets with an eye towards global diversification
